coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient microwave-assisted Fenton degradation of metacycline using pine-needle-like CuCo2O4 nanocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Yi
Mei, Yuqing
Li, Jiaqi
Yao, Tongjie
Yang, Yang
Jia, Wenjie
Tong, Xin
Wu, Jie
Xin, Baifu
coredata.dc:description:
The heterogeneous Fenton reaction was a powerful technique for degrading organics in wastewater. However, it usually took a long time to meet efficiency requirement. To solve this problem, herein, pine-needle-like CuCo2O4 was prepared and applied as a catalyst in a microwave-assisted Fenton reaction (MAFR) towards antibiotic metacycline (MTC). The optimized degradation condition was 500 μL of H2O2, 10.0 mg of catalyst, 50 mg/L of MTC, and 90 °C of temperature, under which MTC was degraded within 4.0 min. Dynamics data revealed that the activation energy of MAFR was 17.3 kJ/mol, which was much lower than that of the corresponding heterogeneous Fenton reaction (52.2 kJ/mol). Owing to the synergistic effect of combining Cu(II)/Cu(I) and Co(III)/Co(II) sites, the reaction rate constant of CuCo2O4 nanoneedles (7.2 × 10−1 min−1) was much higher than that of the CuO (1.3 × 10−1 min−1), Co3O4 (2.2 × 10−1 min−1), and mixed CuO + Co3O4 (1.5 × 10−1 min−1) references. CuCo2O4 nanoneedles could be easily separated from the reaction solution, and their degradation efficiency remained 86.4% after the 5th cycle. To study the catalytic mechanism, radical trapping experiments were performed, and the results revealed that
OH played a dominating role in the degradation process. A possible degradation pathway of MTC was investigated, and it was determined that hydroxylation, de-alkylation, de-hydroxylation, and ring-opening reactions were involved for final inorganic molecules.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient microwave-assisted Fenton degradation of metacycline using pine-needle-like CuCo2O4 nanocatalyst

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pine-needle-like CuCo2O4 nanocatalysts were prepared, and they displayed superior catalytic performance and reusability in a microwave-assisted Fenton reaction towards antibiotic metacycline.

The heterogeneous Fenton reaction was a powerful technique for degrading organics in wastewater. However, it usually took a long time to meet efficiency requirement. To solve this problem, herein, pine-needle-like CuCo2O4 was prepared and applied as a catalyst in a microwave-assisted Fenton reaction (MAFR) towards antibiotic metacycline (MTC). The optimized degradation condition was 500 μL of H2O2, 10.0 mg of catalyst, 50 mg/L of MTC, and 90 °C of temperature, under which MTC was degraded within 4.0 min. Dynamics data revealed that the activation energy of MAFR was 17.3 kJ/mol, which was much lower than that of the corresponding heterogeneous Fenton reaction (52.2 kJ/mol). Owing to the synergistic effect of combining Cu(II)/Cu(I) and Co(III)/Co(II) sites, the reaction rate constant of CuCo2O4 nanoneedles (7.2 × 10−1 min−1) was much higher than that of the CuO (1.3 × 10−1 min−1), Co3O4 (2.2 × 10−1 min−1), and mixed CuO + Co3O4 (1.5 × 10−1 min−1) references. CuCo2O4 nanoneedles could be easily separated from the reaction solution, and their degradation efficiency remained 86.4% after the 5th cycle. To study the catalytic mechanism, radical trapping experiments were performed, and the results revealed that
OH played a dominating role in the degradation process. A possible degradation pathway of MTC was investigated, and it was determined that hydroxylation, de-alkylation, de-hydroxylation, and ring-opening reactions were involved for final inorganic molecules.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decades, antibiotics have been used widely in the prevention and treatment of human and animal diseases [1,2]. Because of high stability and toxicity, it is very difficult to degrade antibiotics by traditional techniques, which results in rapid accumulation and serious environmental issues [3,4]. For example, tetracycline, which is the second most produced and used antibiotic, has been detected in surface water at concentrations as high as 0.07–1.34 µg/L in some places [5]. This presents threats to the ecosystem, and poses risks for human health in the long term. Therefore, it is urgent to establish an effective approach for disposing antibiotics.

Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) are efficient and extensively used technologies for water purification [6–9]. The Fenton reaction is a typical AOP and has unparalleled advantages in the degradation of persistent organic pollutants, in which
OH is produced from the activation of H2O2. The traditional heterogeneous Fenton reaction is initiated by Fe3+/Fe2 and has some shortcomings [10–13], such as poor recyclability, narrow range of applicable pH, and production of iron sludge. These drawbacks greatly restrict the wide application of homogeneous Fenton reaction. At present, the heterogeneous Fenton reaction has attracted increasing attention [14–16]. However, the heterogeneous Fenton reaction also has drawbacks, and the major disadvantage is that the long time is needed to meet the efficiency requirement (in general above 30 min) [17,18]. To accelerate the reaction rate, external energy, such as UV–Vis light [19], electricity [20], ultrasound and microwave (MW), are usually introduced into the system [21–23].

Among them, MW-irradiation can remarkably enhance pollutant degradation, because MW energy can be selectively adsorbed by a catalyst and can produce ‘‘hotpots” to generate extra radicals [24]. Additionally, the non-thermal effect of the MW causes excitation of the pollutant molecules to higher vibrational and rotational energy levels, increasing their activity [25]. Many transition metal oxides have been developed as catalysts for the MW-assisted Fenton reaction (MAFR), such as Fe3O4, Co3O4, Bi2O2CO3, and Mn2CoO4 [26–29]. By using these catalysts, the degradation process can be completed within 10.0 min. In MAFR, H2O2 is mainly activated on the catalyst surface; and hence, the surface area plays a vital role in determining catalytic activity. It is commonly known that nanomaterials have a higher surface area than their bulk-phase counterparts; therefore, it is necessary to shape catalysts into nanostructures to further promote catalytic performance.

On the basis of above ideas, herein, we reported the synthesis of pine-needle-like CuCo2O4 composed of small nanoparticles (NPs) using a hydrothermal reaction followed by calcination. Compared with binary metal oxides, the Cu(II)/Cu(I) and Co(III)/Co(II) active sites were integrated in ternary CuCo2O4, and this led to richer redox properties and more feasible oxidation states. Metacycline (MTC) was a derivative of tetracycline (Fig. S1), and was widely used to treat bacterial infection. Herein, the kinetics and thermodynamics of CuCo2O4 nanoneedles were evaluated via degradation of MTC under different experimental conditions. Their superior catalytic activity was demonstrated by comparing the degradation performance of CuCo2O4 nanoneedles with that of the CuO, Co3O4, and mixed CuO + Co3O4 references. The reusability was tested via cycle experiments and structural analysis. Radical trapping experiments were carried out to explore a possible catalytic mechanism. Because the decomposition of MTC has seldom been reported, the degradation pathway was also investigated.

Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Co(NO3)3·9H2O, urea, ethanol, tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), H2O2 (30 wt%), MTC, vitamin C (VC), polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP, M n = 4 × 104), benzoquinone (BQ), ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), o-phenylendiamine (OPDA) were purchased from China Pharmaceutical Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals are analytical grade and used as received. The water used was purified through a Millipore system with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ·cm−1.

In brief, Cu(NO3)2·9H2O (0.12 g) and Co(NO3)2·9H2O (0.3 g) were first dissolved in deionized water (35 mL). Urea (0.42 g) and PVP (0.05 g) were then added under magnetic stirring. The homogeneous solution was then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and heated at 120 °C. After reaction for a certain amount of time, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally to ambient temperature. The obtained products were thoroughly washed by ethanol and H2O, and dried in an oven at 60 °C for a night. Finally, the dried sample was annealed in air at 300 °C for 4.0 h with a heating rate of 2.0 °C/min, and the final CuCo2O4 nanoneedles were obtained. For comparison, Co3O4 and CuO references were synthesized by the similar recipe except only in the presence of Co(NO3)2·9H2O or Cu(NO3)2·9H2O.

Typically, MTC solution (50 mL, 50 mg/L) and CuCo2O4 nanoneedles (10 mg) were dispersed in a round-bottom flask. The flask was then transferred into a MW oven that had a power of 400 W, temperature of 90 °C, and frequency of 2450 MHz (MAS-Ⅱ, SINEO Microwave Chemistry Technology Co., Ltd, China). After H2O2 (500 μL) was added and the equipment was turned on, MAFR was initiated. In each experiment, eight samples (3.0 mL of each sample) were taken from the reaction solution at specified time intervals. After removing the catalyst by centrifugation, the supernate was immediately measured by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer to monitor the degradation process (Labtech UV-9100B).

To verify the degradation products of MTC (50 mg/ L) in MAFR, 2.0 mL of suspension was collected at predetermined time intervals, and filtered through 0.22 μm Millipore filters to remove the catalyst. The degradation intermediates were identified by high performance liquid chromatography coupled to micro TOF-Q II time-of-flight mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS, Agilent 1260 HPLC/Bruker microTOF-Q II). HPLC was performed on a Waters E2968 instrument equipped with a UV detector and a 4.6 × 250 mm Zorbax Eclipse XDB C18 reverse-phase column at 25 °C. The detection wavelength of the UV detector was 354 nm and the injection volume was 20 μL. The isocratic mobile phase was made up of 25% methanol and 75% water (containing 0.2% formic acid) with a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. The source used was the electrospray ionization positive ion mode. The resulting protonated ions ([M + H+]) were analyzed in the m/z range of 50–600.

The crystalline phase of the CuCo2O4 was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Smartlab apparatus (CuKα radiation, λ = 1.5418 Å, 2θ = 10–80°). Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) was employed to determine the functional groups on sample surface (Nicolet Avatar 360). The morphology and microstructure were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S4800), transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), high-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM), and elemental mappings. X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) analysis was carried out on a VG ESCALAB MKII X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with Mg Kα excitation (1253.6 eV). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area was obtained by measuring N2 adsorption-desorption with a Beckman Coulter 3100 pore size and surface area analyzer. The pore size distribution was calculated from the desorption branch using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method. The sample was degassed at 120 °C for 4.0 h before the isotherm was determined. Total organic carbon (TOC) evolution was monitored to evaluate the mineralization efficiency on a TOC analyzer (multi N/C 2100S, Analytik Jena, Germany). 3.0 mL of sampled solution was diluted to 10.0 mL, and the solution was used for TOC test and pipeline cleaning. A fluorescence photometer (Perkin Elmer LS55) was used to test the concentration change of H2O2 at 90 °C. Inductively coupled plasma (ICP) atomic spectroscopy was performed on an Optima 7000 DV.

Fig. 1 shows XRD patterns of the CuCo2O4 nanoneedles obtained at various hydrothermal reaction times. There are nine characteristic diffraction peaks located at 2θ = 19.0, 31.3, 36.8, 38.6, 44.9, 55.8, 59.3, 65.4 and 77.6°, corresponding to the (1 1 1), (2 2 0), (3 1 1), (2 2 2), (4 0 0), (4 2 2), (5 1 1), (4 4 0), and (5 5 3) crystal planes (JCPDS No. 71–0816). Cu(OH)2 was more likely to precipitate than Co(OH)2 at 278 K (solubility product constants of Cu(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 were 4.8 × 10−20 and 1.6 × 10−15, respectively). When reaction time is short (2.0 h), a weak peak at 35.4° is attributed to (0 0 2) plane of CuO derived from thermal decomposition of Cu(OH)2 [30]. When reaction time reaches 4.0 h, this peak is almost invisible due to the increase of CuCo2O4 content in products. Further extending the reaction time to 6.0 or 8.0 h, the diffraction peak at 35.4° is detected again, revealing the partial decomposition of CuCo2O4. In light of the XRD data, the sample synthesized at 4.0 h was selected as model for MAFR due to purity.

For comparison, the CuO and Co3O4 references are prepared, and their SEM images and XRD patterns are shown in Figs. S2 and S3, respectively. The diffraction peaks in Co3O4 are very close to those in CuCo2O4. To distinguish them, unit cell dimensions (a 0) are calculated according to Eq. (1), where d is the d-spacing acquired from the Bragg formula (Eq. (2), here n = 1 and λ = 1.5418 Å), and h, k, and l are Miller indices. Based on the (3 1 1) plane, a 0 for CuCo2O4 is 8.101 Å, which is larger than that of Co3O4 (8.080 Å). Because the radius of Cu2+ (0.73 Å) is larger than that of Co3+ (0.61 Å), the lattice volume will expand after accommodation of Cu2+ at the octahedral sites in spinel structure, leading to a higher a 0 in CuCo2O4 [31]. As seen in FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S4), two absorption peaks intensively located at 563 and 668 cm−1 are indexed to Cu–O bond and Co–O bond, further verifying the formation of CuCo2O4 [32].

The morphology of the CuCo2O4 sample was investigated using SEM. SEM images of CuCo2O4 exhibit a typical needle-like morphology with a sharp tip; this morphology is reminiscent of pine-needles (Fig. 2 a). The average length and diameter of the needles are estimated to be 11 ± 2 μm and 60 ± 5 nm, respectively. In a high-magnified image (Fig. 2b), it is interesting to note that these nanoneedles gather together, and grow in a radial direction from a center to form a three-dimensional cluster-like architecture. The microstructure of the CuCo2O4 nanoneedles is further studied using TEM. Fig. 2c shows a single cluster, and this also verifies that these nanoneedles grow in the radial direction from the core to the exterior and are close together, leaving dozens of macropores. Closer inspection reveals that each needle is composed of numerous NPs that have a mean size of 10 ± 5 nm (Fig. 2d). Many mesopores are visible among these NPs, and these increase the surface area, accelerate the mass transport and provide more active sites for activation of H2O2 in MAFR. HRTEM image manifests the well-resolved periodic lattice fringe distances are about 0.15 and 0.24 nm, indexing to the (5 1 1) and (3 1 1) crystal planes of cubic phase, respectively (Fig. 2e) [31,33]. The HAADF-STEM image and corresponding elemental mappings are shown in Fig. 2f–i. The distributions of Cu, Co and O are well overlapped with each other in one sample, further confirming the formation of CuCo2O4 nanoneedles.

The N2 adsorption isotherm of CuCo2O4 nanoneedles shows a type III curve, indicating a porous nanostructure (Fig. S5). The BET surface area is estimated to be 39 m2/g. BJH pore size distribution with a broad range of 3.0–180 nm indicates the co-existence of mesopores and macropores (inset of Fig. S5), which is in a good agreement of SEM and TEM observation. Adsorption experiments are performed to further confirm the high surface area of CuCo2O4 nanoneedles (Fig. S6). 47.1% of MTC is adsorbed by CuCo2O4 nanoneedles within 90 min. For comparison, the adsorption efficiencies of CuO, Co3O4 and CuO + Co3O4 were 22.8%, 7.6%, and 15.8%, respectively. The uniform porous structure is the origin of the high surface area, and contributes to the enhanced adsorption efficiency. Only micro-sized aggregates are observed in SEM images of the CuO and Co3O4 samples (Fig. S2), and such aggregates lead to the relatively low adsorption abilities of these samples.

The degradation efficiencies of MAFR using the CuCo2O4 catalyst under different experimental conditions were evaluated. First, the effect of H2O2 on degradation efficiencies was investigated. As seen in Fig. 3 a, when the H2O2 dosage increases from 100 to 300 and to 500 μL, the degradation efficiency at 4.0 min is 43.6%, 54.3% and 95.1%, respectively. With a further increase to 700 μL, the degradation efficiency decreases to 73.3%, because of the inhibitory effect of overusing H2O2 for
OH (Eqs. (3) and (4)) [34]. Therefore, an H2O2 volume of 500 μL was selected.
(3)
OH+H2O2→
OOH+H2O
(4)
OH+OOH→
O2+H2O

The influence of MTC concentrations on degradation efficiency is investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 3b. When the concentration is low (20 or 50 mg/L), the degradation curves nearly overlap, which suggests that the amount of generated reactive oxygen species (ROSs) is very high, and the effect of the MTC concentrations on the degradation rates can be ignored. The degradation rates monotonically decrease with a further increase in the MTC concentrations to 100 and 200 mg/L. In light of collision theory, more MTC occupied a larger catalyst surface, and this retarded the contact between the catalyst and H2O2 for ROS generation; therefore, the degradation rate was lower. Herein, an MTC concentration of 50 mg/L was selected as a standard.

Next, the influence of catalyst dosages is investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 3c. The degradation efficiencies at 1.0 min increase remarkably from 38.4% to 89.1%, when the dosages are in the range of 5.0–12.0 mg. With a further increase to 15.0 mg, the degradation efficiency only slightly increases to 92.5%, because overusing catalysts result in aggregation and an overlapping of active sites. Using the above data, the best dosage of CuCo2O4 nanoneedle was 12.0 mg. Nevertheless, the degradation rate of MTC was too fast to be monitored under this condition. Therefore, a standard catalyst dosage of 10.0 mg was selected, and MTC was degraded within 4.0 min.

Finally, the effect of temperatures on MTC degradation is studied, and the results are shown in Fig. 3d. Pseudo-first-order equation and pseudo-second-order equation were selected to fit the reaction rate constant k at various temperatures. As seen in Fig. S7, Pseudo-first-order kinetic model is more suitable due to high R 2. For temperatures of 40, 60, 80, and 90 °C, the values of k are 5.0 × 10−2, 7.2 × 10−2, 3.9 × 10−1 and 7.2 × 10−1 min−1, respectively. Apparently, the degradation rates increase with respect to an increase in temperature. The reaction temperature was set at 90 °C in this study. It was well known that H2O2 was a thermo-labile compound. Therefore, decomposition efficiency under high temperature was an important issue that needed to be addressed. OPDA was selected as a fluorescence probe to monitor the decomposition process at 90 °C in the absence of a catalyst [35]. As seen in Fig. S8, 67.6% of H2O2 (about 340 μL) still remains after 5.0 min, and this is enough for MAFR.

Using the Arrhenius equation (Eq. (5), R is 8.314 J/mol·K) [36], the activation energy (E a) of MAFR is calculated to be 17.3 kJ/mol (Fig. S9a). For comparison, MTC degradation in a heterogeneous Fenton reaction was conducted without MW-irradiation; the temperature of the reaction was controlled using a water-bath. As seen in Fig. S10, at 90 °C, the degradation rate is about 2.0 × 10−1 min−1, which is much slower than that of MAFR at the same temperature. The E a of the heterogeneous Fenton reaction is 52.2 kJ/mol (Fig. S9b), which is about 3.0 times higher than that in MAFR. It was commonly known that a lower value of E a indicated a higher degradation rate; therefore, coupling MW with Fenton reaction has significantly improved the degradation rate. Thermodynamic parameters in MAFR and the heterogeneous Fenton reaction, such as the enthalpy change, entropy change, and Gibbs free energy change, were calculated according to the Van’t Hoff equation and the Gibbs free energy equation (Eqs. (6) and (7)). The results are shown in Table 1 and indicate that these two reactions are endothermic and non-spontaneous.

To demonstrate the superior catalytic activity of the CuCo2O4 nanoneedles, the CuO and Co3O4 references were prepared, and their degradation rates were tested. As seen in Figs. 4 and S11, the degradation rates of binary CuO and Co3O4 are 1.3 × 10−1 and 2.2 × 10−1 min−1, which are respectively 5.5 and 3.3 times lower than that of CuCo2O4 nanoneedles (7.2 × 10−1 min−1). Obviously, a synergistic effect is generated by coupling Cu(II)/Cu(I) and Co(III)/Co(II) active sites. A physical mixture of CuO + Co3O4 is prepared to further investigate the synergistic effect, and degradation rate of 1.5 × 10−1 min−1 is determined, which is 4.8 times lower than that of CuCo2O4 nanoneedles. Apparently, formation of ternary metal oxides was an effective approach for maximizing the catalytic activity of two metal active sites, because they had a richer redox property than a mixture of binary metal oxides.

To further understand the high catalytic performance of the CuCo2O4 nanoneedles, the MTC concentration was increased to 500 mg/L and catalyst dosage was increased to 20 mg; other parameters were the same as the optimal condition. Under this condition, 97.4% of MTC is degraded within 5.0 min (Fig. S12). In addition, the mineralization efficiency reaches 83.8% within 5.0 min, and this indicates that most of the MTC are mineralized into nontoxic H2O and CO2.

Energy consumption in MAFR was calculated according to Eq. (8) [24], where U E was the energy consumption, P was the power of the MW oven, t was the reaction time, and n was the number of moles of degraded MTC. Under the optimal condition, the energy consumption in MAFR was about 5.8 × 102 KWh/mol. Because of the rapid degradation, this value was lower than that of other techniques for MTC degradation, including electro-Fenton reaction (1.4 × 104 KWh/mol) [37], electrochemical oxidation (2.8 × 104 KWh/mol) [38], and photocatalysis (1.1 × 104 KWh/mol) [39].

The experimental conditions were varied to explore the MAFR mechanism, and the possible pathways for generating ROSs. As seen in Fig. 5 a, in the presence of MW + H2O2, 66.7% of MTC is degraded because of the self-decomposition of H2O2 (Eq. (9)). In the presence of CuCo2O4 + H2O2, 67.8% of MTC is degraded in a heterogeneous Fenton reaction (Eqs. (10)–(13)), and in the presence of MW + CuCo2O4, 77.3% of MTC is degraded. With the help of hot-spots, e − in the conduction band (CB) and h + in the valence band (VB) were separated and reacted with adsorbed H2O or O2 to produce ROSs (Eq. (14) and (15)) [24,28]. Therefore, the ROSs could be generated via at least three routes, including self-decomposition of H2O2, catalytic activation of H2O2 on the CuCo2O4 surface, and reaction between adsorbed –OH group and H2O with h + and e −.

The roles of generated radicals were determined from radical trapping experiments. VC was a well-known scavenger for all ROSs [40], and was introduced into the reaction. The degradation efficiency is only 25.4%, indicating that MAFR is a typical AOP (Fig. 5b). After addition of TBA (scavenger of
OH) [27], the MTC degradation efficiency decreases significantly to 53.2% compared to the blank experiment (95.2%), and this indicates that
OH is one of the active radicals. We also note that the degradation process is not completely quenched, and this indicates
OH is not the only radical in MAFR; there must be other active radicals involved in MTC decomposition. Similar results are obtained after the addition of BQ (scavenger of
O2 −) [41], demonstrating that
O2 − also plays some roles in the degradation process (77.2% at 4.0 min). EDTA is added to the reaction solution as a scavenger to investigate the contribution of h +. [42]. The degradation efficiency reduces to 82.1% at 4.0 min, and this suggests that h + is produced and involved into the degradation reaction. From the scavenger tests, at least three active radicals played joint roles:
O2 − and h + made small contributions than
OH. The contributions of these three radicals followed the order:
OH >
O2 − ≈ h +.

A possible degradation mechanism of the CuCo2O4 nanoneedles in MAFR is presented in Fig. 6 . Because of the special pine-needle-like structure, the MW is multi-reflected among the CuCo2O4 nanoneedles, leading to increased utilization. The main paths for the generation of ROSs include: (i) generation of
OH via self-decomposition of H2O2 (Eq. (9)). (ii) Generation of
OH (+2.8 V vs. NHE) and
OOH (+0.87 V vs. NHE) at both Cu(II)/Cu(I) and Co(III)/Co(II) in CuCo2O4 via a heterogeneous Fenton route (Eqs. (10)–(13)) [43]. (iii) e − is exited to CB (+0.72 V vs. NHE) from ground state (Eqs. (14) and (15)) [44], leaving h + in the VB (+2.21 V vs. NHE) with the help of hot-spots on CuCo2O4 surface. Because the CB position is higher than the potential of O2/
O2 − (−0.33 V vs. NHE) [45], e − cannot reduce the adsorbed O2 to
O2 −. In MAFR,
O2 − is generated via decomposition of
OOH (Eq. (16)) [41]. The VB position is between the potential of
OH/H2O (+2.72 eV vs. NHE) and the potential of
OH/OH (+1.99 eV vs. NHE), indicating h + is powerful enough to oxidize –OH group to
OH (Eq. (17)) [46], whereas the adsorbed H2O on the catalyst surface cannot be oxidized. Additionally, h + itself directly attacks MTC because of its powerful oxidizability. On the basis of above speculation,
OH could be produced via three routes; whereas
O2 − was only generated in the decomposition of
OOH, and h + was only generated via separation of e − and h +. Therefore, in MAFR, these three radicals had a synergistic effect.
OH acted the main roles, because of its high oxidizability and multi-generation paths.

An important issue with regard to the practical application of catalysts was the reusability in the operating process. Herein, the reusability of CuCo2O4 nanoneedles was investigated via repeated use in MAFR. The results in Fig. 7 show that CuCo2O4 nanoneedles can be successfully reused for 5 successive cycles with 86.4% degradation efficiency within a reaction time of 5.0 min. The XRD pattern of the reclaimed sample reveals that the crystal structure of CuCo2O4 nanoneedles is unchanged compared to that of the fresh sample (Fig. S13).

To further test the changes in the surface composition after the 5th cycle, XPS analysis is carried out and the corresponding results are presented in Fig. 8 . In the survey spectra (Fig. 8a), the signals of Cu, Co, C, and O are clearly observed, and their binding energies are the same as in the fresh sample [47]. The amount of C decreases from 12% in original sample to 7.0%, and this indicates that some ligands anchored on catalyst surface are decomposed. Fig. 8b shows the core-level spectra of Cu 2p3/2; two peaks at 932.7 and 934.3 eV are assigned to Cu (I) and Cu (II), respectively [48], and there is a satellite peak at 942.1 eV. In the degradation reaction, both Cu (I) and Cu (II) act as active sites for generating ROSs. Because of the presence of these radicals, some of the CuCo2O4 surface is oxidized, and this leads to an increase of Cu (II) from 41% to 57%. The high-resolution Co 2p3/2 spectra are given in Fig. 8c. In the spectrum of fresh CuCo2O4 nanoneedles, two main doublets with peak positions at 779.2 and 780.6 eV are ascribed to Co (II) and Co (III) sites, respectively [49]. Their relative contributions to the overall Co intensity are 57% and 43%, respectively. After reaction, the peak positions are only slightly changed; however, the amount of Co (III) increases from 43% to 48% due to the oxidization of ROSs. As seen in Fig. 8d, the O 1s peak that is observed at 531.0 eV undergoes some changes. The core-level spectra can be curve-fitted into three components: lattice oxygen O in CuCo2O4 at 529.3 eV (O1), O in the –OH group at 531.0 eV (O2) and O in adsorbed water at 532.2 eV (O3) [47]. In the fresh sample, O1 accounts for 53% of all of the surface oxygen. After reaction, the amount dramatically decreases to 40%; in contrast, the O2 increases significantly from 32% to 42%, and O3 slightly increases from 15% to 18%. The increases in O2 and O3 indicate that the CuCo2O4 surface is strongly hydroxylated after degradation, and this is favorable for catalytic decomposition and radical generation. The potential of h + in the VB of CuCo2O4 is powerful enough to oxidize –OH group to
OH [50], but cannot oxidize adsorbed H2O (Fig. 6). This leads to rapid accumulation of –OH group on the catalyst surface in comparison with a slight increase in H2O, and this further confirms the proposed catalytic mechanism.

Leaching concentrations of Cu2+ and Co3+ were measured using ICP. After the 5th cycle, 0.31 wt% Co3+ and 0.37 wt% Cu2+ were leached from the CuCo2O4 nanoneedles. A homogeneous reaction was initiated using metallic nitrates (the concentrations of Cu2+ and Co3+ were the same as the concentrations of leaching ions). To better demonstrate the effect of homogenous MAFR initiated by metal ions, the degradation process in the absence of metal ions was first measured. As seen in the Fig. 5a (blue line), 66.7% of MTC is degraded by self-decomposition of H2O2 under MW-irradiation. Although the inhibitory effect of NO3 − can suppress the degradation process (see below), only 40.6% of MTC is degraded within 5 min in the homogeneous process (Fig. S14), and this suggests that homogeneous MAFR plays a limited role in MTC degradation.

In natural waters, inorganic anions were ubiquitous and had a remarkable influence on MAFR, because the dielectric constant and ionic strength of the medium were changed. More importantly, these anions reacted with radicals and interfered with the degradation of MTC. Experiments to evaluate the effect of inorganic anions on the MTC degradation were undertaken. As seen in Fig. S15, after addition of NO3 − and HCO3 −, the degradation efficiencies decrease to 52.9% and 43.9%, respectively, within 5.0 min. This is because of production of NO3
and CO3
with low oxidizability [51], suggesting the inhibitory effect of NO3 − and HCO3 − in the Fenton reaction. Conversely, after Cl− and H2PO4 − are introduced, the degradation processes are accelerated due to the formation of Cl2
− and H2PO4
, which have more powerful oxidizability [52,53].

HPLC-MS was conducted to detect the possible degradation pathway of MTC. As seen in time-dependent evolution of the HPLC diagrams (Fig. S16), the intensity of the MTC peak at 5.8 min gradually decreases with an increase in reaction time, and completely vanishes after approximately 2.0 min. Along with the degradation, small molecules with high polarity are produced [54], as indicated by the appearance of four new peaks with low retention times of 5.3, 4.7, 3.4, and 2.8 min. After MTC degradation for 1.0–4.0 min, the MS total ion current is analyzed to identify the intermediates (Fig. S17). From the results, a possible degradation pathway for MTC is proposed and is shown in Fig. 9 . First, large molecule intermediates with m/z = 461.15 and 481.18 are generated via hydroxylation, and this further indicates that
OH is the dominating reactive radical, which is in agreement with the radical trapping experiments [39,55]. Then, the intermediates with m/z = 437.19, 426.14, 416.12 and 392.10 appear, because of the subsequent de-alkylation and de-hydroxylation. A ring-opening reaction then takes place, and this leads to the formation of compounds with m/z = 363.13, 301.10, 294.17, 232.11, and 255.15 [37,56,57]. As the reaction proceeds, functional groups on intermediates continue to be lost, and small molecules, including acids and alcohols (m/z = 164.08, 106.08, 102.10 and 120.01), are produced. Finally, CO2, H2O and inorganic ions are ultimately converted in MAFR, and MTC is successfully degraded into small inorganic molecules.

In this study, pine-needle-like CuCo2O4 catalyst was prepared via a hydrothermal reaction followed by calcination. Nanoneedles were composed of numerous nanoparticles, leading to a mesoporous structure with a surface area of 39 m2/g. In MAFR, the CuCo2O4 nanoneedles displayed superior catalytic activity and MTC was degraded within 4.0 min. The reaction rate was 5.5, 3.3 and 4.8 times higher than that of the CuO, Co3O4, and mixed CuO + Co3O4 references, and this indicated that there was a synergistic effect of integrating Cu(II)/Cu(I) and Co(III)/Co(II). Compared to the heterogeneous Fenton reaction, the CuCo2O4 nanoneedles lowered the activation energy by a factor of 3.0 via coupling with MW-irradiation, and this indicated that the CuCo2O4 nanoneedles were effective catalysts in MAFR. Besides high catalytic activity, the nanoneedles also displayed good reusability and had a degradation efficiency of 86.4% even after the 5th cycle. Additionally, XRD and XPS measurements showed that there were no obvious structural changes after reaction. Radical trapping experiments revealed that
OH,
O2 –, and h + all played joint roles in the degradation process, and the contributions of these species followed the order:
OH>
O2 – ≈ h +. Because there were few studies on the degradation of MTC, a possible degradation pathway was proposed from analysis of intermediates using HLCP-MS.
